questions.question: Define electric current and state its SI unit.
questions.answer: Electric current is the rate of flow of electric charge through a conductor. Its SI unit is ampere (A).

questions.question: Explain electric potential difference.
questions.answer: Electric potential difference is the work done to move a unit charge from one point to another in an electric circuit. It is measured in volts (V).

questions.question: State Ohm’s law.
questions.answer: Ohm’s law states that the current flowing through a conductor is directly proportional to the potential difference across its ends, provided temperature remains constant (V = IR).

questions.question: Define resistance and state its SI unit.
questions.answer: Resistance is the opposition offered by a conductor to the flow of electric current. Its SI unit is ohm (Ω).

questions.question: Write the relation between resistance, resistivity, length, and area of a conductor.
questions.answer: The relation is R = ρL/A, where R is resistance, ρ is resistivity, L is length, and A is cross-sectional area.

questions.question: What is resistivity?
questions.answer: Resistivity is the property of a material that indicates how strongly it resists the flow of electric current.

questions.question: Explain the effect of length of a conductor on resistance.
questions.answer: Resistance increases with increase in length of the conductor because electrons face more opposition while moving.

questions.question: Explain the effect of cross-sectional area on resistance.
questions.answer: Resistance decreases when the cross-sectional area increases because more electrons can flow through the conductor easily.

questions.question: Explain the combination of resistors in series.
questions.answer: In series combination, resistors are connected end-to-end and the same current flows through each resistor. Total resistance is the sum of individual resistances.

questions.question: Explain the combination of resistors in parallel.
questions.answer: In parallel combination, resistors are connected across the same potential difference and current divides among them.

questions.question: State one advantage of parallel combination in household circuits.
questions.answer: Each appliance receives the same voltage and can operate independently.

questions.question: Define electric power.
questions.answer: Electric power is the rate at which electrical energy is consumed or converted in a circuit.

questions.question: Write the formula for electric power.
questions.answer: Electric power is given by P = VI.

questions.question: Write two alternative formulas for electric power using Ohm’s law.
questions.answer: Electric power can also be written as P = I²R and P = V²/R.

questions.question: What is the commercial unit of electrical energy?
questions.answer: The commercial unit of electrical energy is kilowatt-hour (kWh).

questions.question: Define heating effect of electric current.
questions.answer: The heating effect of electric current is the conversion of electrical energy into heat energy when current flows through a conductor.

questions.question: State Joule’s law of heating.
questions.answer: Joule’s law states that heat produced in a conductor is proportional to the square of current, resistance, and time (H = I²Rt).

questions.question: Why is tungsten used in electric bulb filaments?
questions.answer: Tungsten has a very high melting point and can withstand high temperatures without melting.

questions.question: Why are copper and aluminium used for electrical wires?
questions.answer: They are good conductors of electricity with low resistivity.

questions.question: What is a fuse?
questions.answer: A fuse is a safety device that protects an electrical circuit by melting when excessive current flows.

questions.question: Name the device used to measure electric current.
questions.answer: Ammeter.

questions.question: Name the device used to measure potential difference.
questions.answer: Voltmeter.

questions.question: Why should an ammeter be connected in series in a circuit?
questions.answer: An ammeter must be connected in series so that the same current flowing in the circuit passes through it.

questions.question: Why should a voltmeter be connected in parallel?
questions.answer: A voltmeter is connected in parallel to measure potential difference across a component.

questions.question: What happens to current if resistance increases while voltage remains constant?
questions.answer: According to Ohm’s law, if resistance increases, the current decreases.
